title: External call protected by reentrancy guard
benign_code: function withdraw(uint256 a) external nonReentrant { (bool ok,) = msg.sender.call{value:a}(""); require(ok); balances[msg.sender]-=a; }
why_safe: The nonReentrant modifier (OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard or an inline mutex) makes the re-entrant path unreachable, so the external-call-before-state-update ordering cannot be exploited.
detection_hint: Presence of nonReentrant / a _locked mutex modifier on the function (or contract inherits ReentrancyGuard).

title: Checks-Effects-Interactions ordering
benign_code: function withdraw(uint256 a) external { require(balances[msg.sender]>=a); balances[msg.sender]-=a; (bool ok,)=msg.sender.call{value:a}(""); require(ok); }
why_safe: State is updated BEFORE the external call (Effects before Interactions), so a re-entrant call sees the already-decremented balance and cannot drain.
detection_hint: The state mutation to the relevant mapping/variable occurs on a line before the external call.

title: Privileged function guarded by access modifier
benign_code: function setOwner(address n) external onlyOwner { owner = n; }
why_safe: The onlyOwner (or role-based) modifier reverts for non-privileged callers, so the state-changing function is not unprotected.
detection_hint: Function carries onlyOwner / onlyRole / a require(msg.sender==owner) check at entry.

title: Arithmetic on Solidity >= 0.8 (built-in checks)
why_safe: Solidity 0.8+ reverts on overflow/underflow by default, so unchecked SafeMath-style wrapping is unnecessary and a plain +/- is not an overflow vulnerability.
detection_hint: pragma >= 0.8.0 AND the arithmetic is not inside an `unchecked { }` block.

title: block.timestamp used only as a coarse time gate
why_safe: Using block.timestamp for a coarse deadline/timelock (minutes/days granularity) is safe; miner timestamp drift (~15s) cannot meaningfully bypass it. It is only dangerous when used as a source of randomness or for sub-second logic.
detection_hint: block.timestamp appears only in a comparison inside require/if for a deadline, not in entropy/RNG or modulo for selection.

title: Token transfer via SafeERC20
benign_code: using SafeERC20 for IERC20; token.safeTransfer(to, amount);
why_safe: SafeERC20 wraps the transfer and reverts on failure / non-standard return, so there is no unchecked-return-value vulnerability.
detection_hint: Call goes through safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom/safeApprove (SafeERC20), not a raw .transfer/.call.

title: Low-level call with checked return value
why_safe: The boolean return of the low-level call is captured and required, so a silently-failing call cannot proceed undetected.
detection_hint: The (bool ok,) result of .call/.delegatecall is consumed by a require/if, not discarded.

title: Block variable not used for randomness
benign_code: uint256 startBlock = block.number; // recorded for accounting, not RNG
why_safe: Reading block.number/block.timestamp for bookkeeping or event metadata is not weak randomness; it is only a vulnerability when used to derive a winner/selection seed.
detection_hint: block.number/timestamp/blockhash is stored or emitted, but never feeds a modulo/selection/lottery decision.

title: Loop bounded by a small constant
benign_code: for (uint256 i = 0; i < MAX_BATCH; i++) { ... } // MAX_BATCH constant, small
why_safe: A loop bounded by a small compile-time constant cannot be driven to an unbounded gas DoS by an attacker; the bound is not attacker-controlled array length.
detection_hint: Loop bound is a constant/immutable small value, not .length of an externally-growable array.

title: Pragma / naming / informational lint, not a vulnerability
benign_code: pragma solidity ^0.8.20; // floating pragma — style, not security-critical here
why_safe: Floating-pragma, naming-convention, and unused-variable lints are code-quality signals, not exploitable vulnerabilities; they should not be counted as HIGH/CRITICAL findings.
detection_hint: Finding type is solc-version/pragma/naming/visibility-style with no asset-flow or auth impact.

title: Front-running mitigated by commit-reveal (or no order-dependent value)
benign_code: function commit(bytes32 h) external { commits[msg.sender]=h; } function reveal(uint256 v, bytes32 salt) external { require(commits[msg.sender]==keccak256(abi.encodePacked(v,salt))); }
why_safe: A commit-reveal scheme hides the value until ordering is fixed, so an observer cannot profitably front-run; likewise a function whose outcome does not depend on transaction order has no front-running advantage. Tools that flag any state-changing public function as front-runnable produce a false positive here.
detection_hint: Two-step commit-then-reveal using keccak256 over a salted value; OR the flagged function transfers no value whose outcome depends on ordering.

title: Short-address attack not exploitable on modern Solidity
benign_code: pragma solidity ^0.5.0; function transfer(address to, uint256 amt) public { ... }
why_safe: The short-address attack relied on clients sending under-length calldata; Solidity >=0.5 reverts on malformed calldata length and modern wallets/ABI encoders left-pad addresses, so the vector is effectively dead. Flagging it on a modern contract is a false positive.
detection_hint: pragma >= 0.5 (calldata-length validation era) or standard ABI encoding; the attack requires a non-conforming client and is mitigated at the encoding layer.
